channel.title: Minimal Blog - @lekoarts/gatsby-theme-minimal-blog
channel.description: A blog of daily 5 min engineering digests

item.title: The self-taught SWE
item.content:encoded:
Last updated: 2020-08-04

I started teaching myself programming at the beginning of 2017 and have taken countless online courses, most of which are free and the rest at a discount. This is a list of resources that I found helpful, broken down to categories where I think will be useful for someone who has just started out.

#To the readers: please reach out to me if you have any recommendations and I will gladly add them to my list. This blog post is meant to help ease others into programming and avoid bombarding them with irrelevant information.

What is programming? How do I start?

There are lots of specializations out there when it comes to programming, as you might have seen a few such as Frontend Developer, Backend Developer, Full Stack Engineer, Security, DevOps, etc. However, we don't need to worry about what they all mean for now, as it's something you will inevitably learn about with more experience in your belt.

Let's start with two of the broadest terms Backend vs Frontend. This should be enough for us to dive into programming with hopefully some fun projects!

Frontend

You can think of frontend as the stuff that makes your websites pretty. Every text, style, color and animation you see is frontend. Of course, there is more to that but we will get there later.

Things to know

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • Javascript

Relevant skills

UI Libraries:

  • React
  • Angular

Frontend framework:

  • Bootstrap
  • Styled components
  • ...

Projects

  • Make your own website with React and host it on gh-pages, AWS or heroku
  • Make an e-commerce website
  • Make a blog page

Resources

FREE:

  • Scrimba

PAID:

  • Node with React Fullstack Web Development
  • Modern React with Redux
  • Complete React Developer Zero to Mastery

BOOKS:

Backend

You can think of backend as all the server and database stuff that powers any website. It handles data ETL and sends/receives data to and from the frontend, whether it is user authentication, data manipulation or more.

Things to know

These are arguably the easiest languages to pick up with lots of documentation online. Try them all but focus on one and try to master it. For reference, most people master either python or java for their coding interviews.

  • Java
  • Python
  • Javascript (Nodejs)

Relevant skills

  • Languages: Python, Java, C++, Go, etc. See job postings.
  • REST
  • Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) such as MySQL, Postgres
  • NoSQL databases such as MongoDB, Firebase, etc.

Projects

  • Make a full stack e-commerce website
  • Implement a distributed system that can handle high load on any cloud provider such as AWS, Azure, GCP

Resources

FREE:

  • Any free MOOC works.
  • Educative.io

PAID:

  • Udemy courses with full-stack projects

BOOKS:

item.title: Training a ML Model to Identify Offensive Speech
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Josh Baron, Nancy Tran, QA Hoang

Problem: Identify hate/offensive speech

  • Goals:
    • Minimize false negatives
    • Improve false positives via user feedback

Dataset:

The initial dataset we used was data from the paper Automated Hate Speech Detection and the Problem of Offensive Language by T. Davison, et al. They searched for tweets that contained words and phrases identified by internet users as hate speech and had them labelled by CrowdFlower workers. The tweets were labelled as hate speech, offensive speech, or neither. Each tweet was labeled 3 times, and the final labeling was the majority label. In this dataset, only 5% of the tweets were labelled as hate speech.

We initially re-labelled the data so that offensive speech and neither were labeled as “non-hate speech” and left the hate speech label as is. When we ran our initial models, the confusion matrix indicated that there were a decent number of false positives and our metrics were pretty low (see confusion matrix and metrics table below).

Import packages

1# nltk library
2import nltk
3nltk.download("stopwords")
4nltk.download("punkt")
5
6from nltk.stem.wordnet import WordNetLemmatizer
7from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize
8from nltk.corpus import stopwords
9from nltk.stem.porter import PorterStemmer
10from nltk import pos_tag
11
12from collections import defaultdict
13
14import pandas as pd
15import numpy as np
16import re   # Regular expressions
17import requests
18from io import StringIO
19import string
20
21# sklearn
22from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
23from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
24from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
25from sklearn.metrics import( 
26    roc_auc_score, 
27    roc_curve,
28    auc, 
29    confusion_matrix,
30    recall_score, 
31    precision_score, 
32    accuracy_score,
33    classification_report,
34    plot_confusion_matrix
35)
36from sklearn.svm import LinearSVC, SVC
37from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold
38from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier
39from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
40from sklearn.ensemble import BaggingClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier, VotingClassifier, StackingClassifier
41from sklearn.preprocessing import minmax_scale, FunctionTransformer
42from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline, FeatureUnion
43from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel
44
45# Misc
46import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
47import textstat
48from scipy.sparse import hstack
49from vaderSentiment.vaderSentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer
50from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
51from joblib import dump, load

Data Preprocessing

Sanitizing data

1def preprocess(tweet: str):
2    space_pattern = '\s+'
3    url_regex = ('http[s]?://(?:[a-zA-Z]|[0-9]|[$-_@.&+]|'
4        '[!*\(\),]|(?:%[0-9a-fA-F][0-9a-fA-F]))+')
5    mention_regex = '@[^\s]+'
6    symbol_regex = '&#[^\s]+'
7    
8    parsed_tweet = tweet.lower()
9    parsed_tweet = re.sub(space_pattern, ' ', parsed_tweet)
10    parsed_tweet = re.sub(url_regex, 'URLHERE', parsed_tweet)
11    parsed_tweet = re.sub(symbol_regex, ' ', parsed_tweet)
12    parsed_tweet = re.sub(mention_regex, 'MENTIONHERE', parsed_tweet)
13    
14    return parsed_tweet
15
16
17def analyzer(doc: str):
18    stemmer = PorterStemmer()
19    words = word_tokenize(doc)
20    filtered_words = [word for word in words if not word in all_stopwords and word.isalnum()]
21    
22    return [stemmer.stem(word) for word in filtered_words if word not in ['URLHERE', 'MENTIONHERE']]
23
24
25def tokenize(tweet):
26    stemmer = PorterStemmer()
27    tweet = " ".join(re.split("[^a-zA-Z]*", tweet.lower())).strip()
28    tokens = [stemmer.stem(t) for t in tweet.split()]
29    return tokens

Adding sentiment analysis feature

1def getSentiment(df):
2    sentiment_analyzer = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()
3    scores = defaultdict(list)
4    for i in range(len(df)):
5        score_dict = sentiment_analyzer.polarity_scores(df[i])
6        scores['neg'].append(score_dict['neg'])
7        scores['neu'].append(score_dict['neu'])
8        scores['pos'].append(score_dict['pos'])
9        scores['compound'].append(score_dict['compound'])
10    return np.array(pd.DataFrame(scores))

Tfidf Vectorizer

1all_stopwords = stopwords.words('english')
2all_stopwords += ['user', '@', '!', 'rt', 'http', 'lol', 'like', 'amp', 'co', 'get', 'ff']
3vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
4    preprocessor=preprocess,
5    stop_words=all_stopwords,
6    min_df=5,
7    max_df=.75,
8    analyzer=analyzer,
9    ngram_range=(2,4),
10    smooth_idf=False,
11    max_features=10000
12)
13
14char_vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
15    tokenizer=tokenize,
16    preprocessor=preprocess,
17    stop_words=all_stopwords,
18    min_df=5,
19    max_df=.5,
20    ngram_range=(3,5),
21    smooth_idf=False,
22    max_features=10000
23)

Combining features with FeatureUnion

1feature_union = FeatureUnion([
2    ('word_vec', vectorizer),
3    ('char_vec', char_vectorizer),
4    ('sentiment', FunctionTransformer(getSentiment))
5])

Training

1X, y = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['tweet']), df['class']
2X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, stratify=y,  test_size=0.3)
3
4### SMOTE
5
6sm = SMOTE(random_state=42)
7X_train_sm, y_train_sm = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
8print(X_train_sm.shape, y_train_sm.shape)
9
10### SMOTE
11
12clf = LinearSVC(max_iter=1000000, class_weight={1: 4.5, 0: 1}, loss='hinge')
13clf.fit(X_train_sm, y_train_sm)
14
15pipeline = Pipeline([
16    ('feature_union', feature_union),
17    ('select_from_model', SelectFromModel(LogisticRegression(max_iter=100000))),
18    ('classifier', clf)
19])
20
21pipeline.fit(df['tweet'], y)

item.title: React Hooks
item.content:encoded:
useState

useEffect

1const [user, setUser] = useState(null);
2const [searchQuery, setSearchQuery] = useState('Jane Doe');
3
4// triggers on any life cycle update
5// takes an array of properties that trigger update
6useEffect(() => {
7    const fetchFunc = async () => {
8        const res = await fetch(``);
9        const resJson = await res.json();
10        setUser(resJson);
11    }
12    fetchFunc();
13}, [searchQuery]); // only runs when searchQuery changes

ComponentDidMount

1//Class
2componentDidMount() {
3    console.log('I just mounted!');
4}
5
6//Hooks
7useEffect(() => {
8    console.log('I just mounted!');
9}, [])

ComponentWillUnmount //Class componentWillUnmount() { console.log('I am unmounting'); }

1//Hooks
2useEffect(() => {
3    return () => console.log('I am unmounting');
4}, [])

ComponentWillReceiveProps //Class componentWillReceiveProps(nextProps) { if (nextProps.count !== this.props.count) { console.log('count changed', nextProps.count); } }

1//Hooks
2useEffect(() => {
3    console.log('count changed', props.count);
4}, [props.count])

custom hook

1// use-fetch.effect.js
2
3import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
4
5const useFetch = (url) => {
6    const [data, setData] = useState(null);
7    useEffect(() => {
8        const fetchData = async () => {
9            const res = await fetch(url);
10            setData(res.json());
11        };
12        fetchData();
13    });
14
15    return data;
16}

useReducer

useContext

useMemo

useCallback

item.title: React Context API
item.content:encoded: <style data-emotion-css="1hycliv">body{--theme-ui-colors-transparent:var(--theme-ui-colors-transparent,transparent);--theme-ui-colors-black:var(--theme-ui-colors-black,#000);--theme-ui-colors-white:var(--theme-ui-colors-white,#fff);--theme-ui-colors-gray-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-gray-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-1,#f7fafc);--theme-ui-colors-gray-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-2,#edf2f7);--theme-ui-colors-gray-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-3,#e2e8f0);--theme-ui-colors-gray-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-4,#cbd5e0);--theme-ui-colors-gray-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-5,#a0aec0);--theme-ui-colors-gray-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-6,#718096);--theme-ui-colors-gray-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-7,#4a5568);--theme-ui-colors-gray-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-8,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-gray-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-gray-9,#1a202c);--theme-ui-colors-red-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-red-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-1,#fff5f5);--theme-ui-colors-red-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-2,#fed7d7);--theme-ui-colors-red-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-3,#feb2b2);--theme-ui-colors-red-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-4,#fc8181);--theme-ui-colors-red-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-5,#f56565);--theme-ui-colors-red-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-6,#e53e3e);--theme-ui-colors-red-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-7,#c53030);--theme-ui-colors-red-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-8,#9b2c2c);--theme-ui-colors-red-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-red-9,#742a2a);--theme-ui-colors-orange-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-orange-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-1,#fffaf0);--theme-ui-colors-orange-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-2,#feebc8);--theme-ui-colors-orange-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-3,#fbd38d);--theme-ui-colors-orange-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-4,#f6ad55);--theme-ui-colors-orange-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-5,#ed8936);--theme-ui-colors-orange-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-6,#dd6b20);--theme-ui-colors-orange-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-orange-7,#c05621);--theme- 61);--theme-ui-colors-teal-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-teal-9,#234e52);--theme-ui-colors-blue-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-blue-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-1,#ebf8ff);--theme-ui-colors-blue-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-2,#bee3f8);--theme-ui-colors-blue-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-3,#90cdf4);--theme-ui-colors-blue-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-4,#63b3ed);--theme-ui-colors-blue-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-5,#4299e1);--theme-ui-colors-blue-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-6,#3182ce);--theme-ui-colors-blue-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-7,#2b6cb0);--theme-ui-colors-blue-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-8,#2c5282);--theme-ui-colors-blue-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-blue-9,#2a4365);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-1,#ebf4ff);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-2,#c3dafe);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-3,#a3bffa);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-4,#7f9cf5);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-5,#667eea);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-6,#5a67d8);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-7,#4c51bf);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-8,#434190);--theme-ui-colors-indigo-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-indigo-9,#3c366b);--theme-ui-colors-purple-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-purple-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-1,#faf5ff);--theme-ui-colors-purple-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-2,#e9d8fd);--theme-ui-colors-purple-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-3,#d6bcfa);--theme-ui-colors-purple-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-4,#b794f4);--theme-ui-colors-purple-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-5,#9f7aea);--theme-ui-colors-purple-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-6,#805ad5);--theme-ui-colors-purple-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-7,#6b46c1);--theme-ui-colors-purple-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-8,#553c9a);--theme-ui-colors-purple-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-purple-9,#44337a);--theme-ui-colors-pink-0:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-0,null);--theme-ui-colors-pink-1:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-1,#fff5f7);--theme-ui-colors-pink-2:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-2,#fed7e2);--theme-ui-colors-pink-3:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-3,#fbb6ce);--theme-ui-colors-pink-4:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-4,#f687b3);--theme-ui-colors-pink-5:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-5,#ed64a6);--theme-ui-colors-pink-6:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-6,#d53f8c);--theme-ui-colors-pink-7:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-7,#b83280);--theme-ui-colors-pink-8:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-8,#97266d);--theme-ui-colors-pink-9:var(--theme-ui-colors-pink-9,#702459);--theme-ui-colors-grayDark:var(--theme-ui-colors-grayDark,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-text:var(--theme-ui-colors-text,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-background:var(--theme-ui-colors-background,#fff);--theme-ui-colors-primary:var(--theme-ui-colors-primary,#6b46c1);--theme-ui-colors-primaryHover:var(--theme-ui-colors-primaryHover,#2c5282);--theme-ui-colors-secondary:var(--theme-ui-colors-secondary,#5f6c80);--theme-ui-colors-muted:var(--theme-ui-colors-muted,#e2e8f0);--theme-ui-colors-success:var(--theme-ui-colors-success,#9ae6b4);--theme-ui-colors-info:var(--theme-ui-colors-info,#63b3ed);--theme-ui-colors-warning:var(--theme-ui-colors-warning,#faf089);--theme-ui-colors-danger:var(--theme-ui-colors-danger,#feb2b2);--theme-ui-colors-light:var(--theme-ui-colors-light,#f7fafc);--theme-ui-colors-dark:var(--theme-ui-colors-dark,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-textMuted:var(--theme-ui-colors-textMuted,#718096);--theme-ui-colors-toggleIcon:var(--theme-ui-colors-toggleIcon,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-heading:var(--theme-ui-colors-heading,#000);--theme-ui-colors-divide:var(--theme-ui-colors-divide,#cbd5e0);color:var(--theme-ui-colors-text,#2d3748);background-color:var(--theme-ui-colors-background,#fff);}body.theme-ui-dark{--theme-ui-colors-text:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-text,#cbd5e0);--theme-ui-colors-primary:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-primary,#9f7aea);--theme-ui-colors-secondary:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-secondary,#7f8ea3);--theme-ui-colors-toggleIcon:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-toggleIcon,#cbd5e0);--theme-ui-colors-background:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-background,#1A202C);--theme-ui-colors-heading:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-heading,#fff);--theme-ui-colors-divide:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-divide,#2d3748);--theme-ui-colors-muted:var(--theme-ui-colors-modes-dark-muted,#2d3748);}</style><style data-emotion-css="tm2q0o">*{box-sizing:border-box;}body{margin:0;font-family:"IBM Plex Sans",-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Roboto,"Helvetica Neue",Arial,"Noto Sans",sans-serif,"Apple Color Emoji","Segoe UI Emoji","Segoe UI Symbol","Noto Color Emoji";line-height:1.625;font-weight:400;color:var(--theme-ui-colors-text,#2d3748);background-color:var(--theme-ui-colors-background,#fff);padding:0;box-sizing:border-box;text-rendering:optimizeLegibility;-webkit-font-smoothing:antialiased;-moz-osx-font-smoothing:grayscale;}</style>

item.title: Django-React Full Stack pt.2
item.content:encoded: React

item.title: Kubernetes
item.content:encoded:
Why Kubernetes?

  • Container Orchestration
  • Deploy services, roll out new releases without downtime. Scale up/down services.
  • Portable
  • Self healing
  • Load balance requests and monitor resources
  • Easy access to logs

kubectl & minikube

Start Minikube to create a cluster

1# create a new VM based on Minikube image
2# image contains Docker, rkt container engines and localkube library
3minikube start --vm-driver=virtualbox
4
5# stop
6minikube stop
7
8# delete cluster: no data or state is preserved
9minikube delete
10
11minikube status
12
13minikube dashboard
14
15# checking env variables
16minikube docker-env

Configuring the shell

1# every command we send to local Docker client will be excecuted on Minikube VM
2eval $(minikube docker-env)

Verification

1docker container ls
2
3minikube ssh
4docker container ls
5exit
6
7kubectl config current-context
8kubectl get nodes
9kubectl get all --all-namespaces

Pods

A Pod encapsulates one or more containers. It provides a unique network IP, attaches storage resources, and also decides how containers should run. Everything in a Pod is tightly coupled.

Create a Pod with Mongo

1kubectl run db --image mongo --generator "run-pod/v1"
2
3# verify
4kubectl get pods
5eval $(minikube docker-env)
6docker container ls -f ancestor=mongo
7
8# delete
9kubectl delete pod db

Define Pods via yaml

1# example - db.yml
2apiVersion: v1
3kind: Pod
4metadata:
5    name: db
6    labels:
7        type: db
8        vendor: MongoLabs
9spec:
10    containers:
11    - name: db
12        image: mongo:3.3
13        command: ["mongod"]
14        args: ["--rest", "--httpinterface"]
15
16# create pod defined in db.yml
17kubectl create -f db.yml
18
19kubectl describe -f db.yml

Components and stages in a Pod's scheduling

  1. API Server

    • Runs on the master node
    • All other components interact with API server and keep watch for changes

  2. Scheduler

    • Runs on the master node
    • Watch for unassigned pods and assign them to a node which has available resources (CPU and memory) matching Pod requirements

  3. Kubelet

    • Runs on each node
    • Makes sure assigned pods are running on the node
    • If a Pod is assigned to the node Kubelet is running on, it pulls the Pod definition and create containers accordingly

item.title: Django-React Full Stack pt.1
item.content:encoded: Django

item.title: Bash Tutorial pt.3
item.content:encoded:
Tricks

  • sort -r : reverse sort
  • sort -f : sort but ignore case

Sort numbers

1sort -n somedata

Cut out parts of output

1# -c option takes particular columns
2# -d option specifies a different delimiter (default = tab character)
3ps -l | cut -c12-15

Removing duplicates

1somesequence | sort -u
2
3somesequence | uniq > myfile

Translate characters

1tr ';' ',' <be.fore >af.ter

Convert uppercase to lowercase

1tr 'A-Z' 'a-z' <be.fore >af.ter
2tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' <be.fore >af.ter

Counting lines, words, characters in a file

1wc data_file
2
3# lines only
4wc -l data_file
5
6# words only
7wc -w data_file
8
9# characters only
10wc -c data_file

item.title: Bash Tutorial pt.2
item.content:encoded:
awk

Shell variables

  • $NF : awk's built in variable that holds number of fields found on current line

Tricks

  • read -a LSL : reads a line at a time and each word is assigned to an entry in the array LSL

awk utility reads data from the filename supplied, or from stdin if no filename is given

1awk '{print $1}' myinput.file
2
3awk '{print $1}' < myinput.file
4
5cat myinput.file | awk '{print $1}'

Reverse words on a line

1$ awk '{
2>    for (i=NF; i>=0; i--) {
3>        printf "%s ", $i;
4>    }
5>    printf "\n"
6> }' <filename>
7
8# one liner
9awk '{for (i=NF; i>=0; i--) {printf "%s ", $i;} printf "\n" }'<filename>

Summing a list of numbers

1# sum up the file sizes from the output of an `ls` command
2ls -l | awk '{sum += $5}; END {print sum}'
3
4# calls `next` command for ^total block
5ls -l | awk '/^total/{next} {sum += $5}; END {print sum}'

Counting string values with bash

1# # cookbook filename: cnt_owner
2# count owners of a file using bash
3# pipe "ls -l" into this script
4
5declare -A AACOUNT
6while read  -a LSL
7do
8    # only consider lines that are 7 words or longer
9    if (( ${#LSL[*]} > 7 ))     # the size of the array
10    then
11        NDX=${LSL[3]}                 # string assign
12        (( AACOUNT[${NDX}] += 1 ))    # math increment
13    fi
14done
15
16for VALS in "${!AACOUNT[@]}"      # index of each element
17do
18    echo $VALS "owns" ${AACOUNT[$VALS]} "files"
19done

item.title: Bash Tutorial pt.1
item.content:encoded:
grep

Shell variables

  • $?: return value after running a command
  • !!: history operator - lets you repeat prev command

The grep command searches through files looking for the expression you supply 

1grep printf *.c

See also

  • man grep
  • man regex (Linux, Solaris) or man re_format (BSD, Mac)

Use filenames found by grep on the command line:

1rm -i $(grep -l 'This file is obsolete' *)

Getting a simple True/False from a searches

1if grep -q findme bigdata.file ; then echo yes ; else echo nope ; fi
2
3# redirecting to /dev/null sends output to a `bit bucket`
4# that just throws away everything you give it
5if grep findme bigdata.file > /dev/null ; then echo yes ; else echo nope ; fi

Filter search results

1grep -i dec logfile | grep -vi decimal | grep -vi decimate

Grep compressed files (zgrep, zcat, gzcat, less)

1zgrep 'search term' /var/log/messages*
2
3# used in covering damaged compressed files
4# output everything it possibily can
5zcat /var/log/messages.1.gz

item.title: Test
item.content:encoded:
Here will a React component go:

Here will a live code example go:

Here will a normal code block go:

1(function() {
2
3var cache = {};
4var form = $('form');
5var minified = true;
6
7var dependencies = {};
8
9var treeURL = 'https://api.github.com/repos/PrismJS/prism/git/trees/gh-pages?recursive=1';
10var treePromise = new Promise(function(resolve) {
11    $u.xhr({
12        url: treeURL,
13        callback: function(xhr) {
14            if (xhr.status < 400) {
15                resolve(JSON.parse(xhr.responseText).tree);
16            }
17        }
18    });
19});

A code block with a JSDoc comment, short, and long comment:

1/**
2 * Get value out of string (e.g. rem => px)
3 * If value is px strip the px part
4 * If the input is already a number only return that value
5 * @param {string | number} input
6 * @param {number} [rootFontSize]
7 * @return {number} Number without last three characters
8 * @example removeLastThree('6rem') => 6
9 */
10const getValue = (input, rootFontSize = 16) => {
11  if (typeof input === `number`) {
12    return input / rootFontSize;
13  }
14
15  const isPxValue = input.slice(-2) === `px`;
16
17  if (isPxValue) {
18    return parseFloat(input.slice(0, -2));
19  }
20
21  return parseFloat(input.slice(0, -3));
22};
23
24// This is a little helper function
25const helper = (a, b) => a + b;
26
27// This is also a little helper function but this time with a really long one-line comment that should show some more details
28const morehelper = (a, b) => a * b;
29
30export { getValue, helper, morehelper };

Normal block without language:

1import Test from "../components/test"
2
3const Layout = ({ children }) => (
4  <Test>
5    {children}
6  </Test>
7)
8
9export default Layout

Code block with code highlighting:

src/components/post.jsx
1import React from "react";
2
3const Post = ({ data: { post } }) => (
4  <Layout>
5    <Heading variant="h2" as="h2">
6      {post.title}
7    </Heading>
8    <p
9      sx={{
10        color: `secondary`,
11        mt: 3,
12        a: { color: `secondary` },
13        fontSize: [1, 1, 2],
14      }}
15    >
16      <span>{post.date}</span>
17      {post.tags && (
18        <React.Fragment>
19          {` — `}
20          <ItemTags tags={post.tags} />
21        </React.Fragment>
22      )}
23    </p>
24    <section
25      sx={{
26        ...CodeStyles,
27        my: 5,
28        ".gatsby-resp-image-wrapper": { my: 5, boxShadow: `lg` },
29      }}
30    >
31      <MDXRenderer>{post.body}</MDXRenderer>
32    </section>
33  </Layout>
34);
35
36export default Post;

Code block without title:

1Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone

Code block without lineNumbers (but lang):

Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets

Code block without lineNumbers (and without lang):

Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets

Code block with only the title:

src/utils/scream.js
1const scream = (input) => window.alert(input)

Code block with only the title but without lineNumbers:

src/utils/scream.js
const scream = (input) => window.alert(input)

Line highlighting without code title:

1const test = 3;
2const foo = "bar";
3const harry = "potter";
4const hermione = "granger";
5const ron = "weasley";

Here will inline code go, just inside the text. Wow!

Code block without line numbers but with highlighting, language, and title:

src/components/blog.tsx
import React from "react";
const Blog = ({ posts }: PostsProps) => {
  const { tagsPath, basePath } = useSiteMetadata();
  return (
    <Layout>
      <Flex sx={{ alignItems: `center`, justifyContent: `space-between` }}>
        <Heading variant="h2" as="h2">
          Blog
        </Heading>
        <Styled.a
          as={Link}
          sx={{ variant: `links.secondary` }}
          to={`/${basePath}/${tagsPath}`.replace(/\/\/+/g, `/`)}
        >
          View all tags
        </Styled.a>
      </Flex>
      <Listing posts={posts} sx={{ mt: [4, 5] }} />
    </Layout>
  );
};
export default Blog;

item.title: How to make this blog
item.content:encoded:
This blog is made with gatsby and follows a custom theme by LekoArts.

Here is a step by step tutorial to make your own blog :)

  1. Clone github repo

    1git clone https://github.com/LekoArts/gatsby-starter-minimal-blog

  2. Change 'Lupin' blog title to your own by adding siteTitle to gatsby-config.js

    1siteMetadata: {
    2    siteTitle: "QA Hoang",
    3}

  3. Deploy to github pages

    • Add deploy script to package.json

      1"deploy": "gatsby build --prefix-paths && gh-pages -d public"

    • Add pathPrefix to gatsby-config.js which points to your github pages repo

      1pathPrefix: "/gatsby-blog"

  4. Add your blog posts in content/pages/posts using markdown

  5. Run npm run deploy and voila!

Happy coding!
